title: Why Orange Success? - Retention and Student Success – Syracuse University
content:
Why Orange Success? - Retention and Student Success – Syracuse University
Orange Success is all about student success! It is a web-based advising tool that improves the way we work to support student success across all schools and colleges at Syracuse University. Orange Success is an information hub, collecting and storing student success information in a single location. Students, faculty and staff can manually raise flags, in addition to an automated process for system triggered flags (alerts). Faculty can support and nurture students through the use of kudos (compliments) for performance improvements and to recognize outstanding contributions. Because success is a moving target and students need different kinds of encouragement at different times, Orange Success will engage students by keeping them informed and prompting them to act when necessary.
You can access Orange Success from MySlice and Blackboard.  There are three primary users—students, faculty and TA’s, and academic advisors, each having a specific role in Orange Success with custom permissions. For example, faculty and/or TAs can raise early alert flags for attendance concerns, low participation, missing assignments, low quiz/test scores, etc., and academic advisors will follow up with the student, address or resolve the issue, and finally, close the loop with the faculty who raised the early alert flag. Faculty can also send kudos (compliments) to students to recognize achievement.  Students can ask for help from faculty in a particular course or from an advisor for non-course specific needs.  The faculty or academic advisor will be informed with an email and will reach out to the student to provide coordinated support and point them to appropriate resources on campus.  It is teamwork in every sense of the word.
The key to student success is faculty—your observations, perspectives, and feedback are a vital part of the student’s journey from start to finish.  The Orange Success platform allows you to easily provide feedback to students with just a few clicks. Your concerns are also viewed by academic advisors who will assist in addressing and/or resolving the issue and close the loop with you. You make an instant impact on that individual student’s success.  You can also send kudos (compliments) to students who are doing well and/or showing improvement and have the ability to communicate with your students individually or in groups.  In Orange Success, you will also find a plethora of information on services available to help students reach their goals.  Also, the Progress Survey is an easy way for faculty to provide input on student progress for an entire class at one time.
Academic Advisors are the front line of student support—you support and challenge students every day to realize their fullest potential.  The Orange Success platform can, at a glimpse, show which students are in need of what kind of help. This advising tool allows you to have a one-click access to a holistic view of a student—past advising activity, flags, courses, grades, demographic data, notes and referrals. You can refer the student to other campus services and keep track to confirm that your recommendation was followed. You can also quickly close the loop by informing faculty, and others who have reported concerns, that the issue was either addressed or resolved. You can access communications, interactions, and meetings with your student in a protected, central location and take advantage of predefined email templates to help you work more efficiently without losing the personal touch.
Syracuse University has given you a home to grow, mature, and become comfortable with yourself.  All of you are going to need support throughout your time in college and the type of support is going to be different for everyone. The Orange Success platform simplifies the way that students, faculty, and staff, connect with each other and this has a powerful impact on your ultimate success. You have the opportunity to request help from your advisor and faculty member regarding a particular course, advising question, or a general concern through the system. Your individual efforts matter…and you can exert significant influence and control over your academic and personal success. Log in and make sure your information on the system is accurate: your email address; your cell number; your preference to receive email or text messages; and upload a new photo if you wish.
from your advisor or faculty member by using the “Request Help” feature in the system. Your efforts matter. You definitely control your success.
Opt-In Method: Students should opt in during the enrollment/new student onboarding process
By providing your mobile number, you agree to receive text messages from Syracuse University’s Center for Student Excellence/Orange Success 2.0 regarding academic support, appointments, and student success resources. Message frequency varies. Reply STOP to opt out or HELP for assistance. Message and data rates may apply. Consent is not required as a condition of enrollment.

= The maximum amount of loan that can be borrowed during a specific academic career or period of time.
= A dependent student is someone whose parent(s) information is required on the FAFSA based on the
dependency status questions
We are still waiting for the final rules on what counts as “enrollment.” However, students should operate under the assumption that “enrollment” means both registration and attendance in a course.
= is a type of federal student loan for eligible graduate and professional students. It is a credit-based loan, and it is not based on the student’s financial need. Students can borrow up to the cost of attendance, minus any other aid the student is receiving.
= A legacy provision in federal student aid refers to rules that allow students or parents with specific eligibility to continue receiving aid or borrowing federal loans under older criteria, even if the criteria have since changed.
= is a federal loan that the parent(s) of a dependent undergraduate student can borrow to help pay for their child’s education. It is a credit-based loan, not based on the student’s or family’s financial need. Parents can borrow up to the cost of attendance, minus any other aid the student is receiving.
= is a type of loan where the government pays the interest on the loan while the borrower is in school at least half-time, during a grace period, and during periods of deferment.
= is a type of loan where the interest accrues during the period of enrollment, during the grace period, and during deferment.
: For students enrolled in an undergraduate program and who had a federal student loan disbursed prior to July 1, 2026, the program of study refers to their credential level at Syracuse University.
If a student enrolled in a bachelor’s program at Syracuse University before July 1, 2026, they may maintain legacy status during the course of their bachelor’s program (even if they change their major).
Similarly, if a student enrolled in an associate’s degree program at Syracuse University before July 1, 2026, they may maintain legacy status during the course of their associate’s degree program, but will not be granted legacy status if they change to a bachelor’s degree program.
If a student enrolled in an associate’s or bachelor’s degree program at Syracuse University before July 1, 2026, and the student transfers out of Syracuse University, they will not maintain legacy status at their new institution.
Program of Study- Graduate/Law
: For students enrolled in a graduate or professional program and who had a federal student loan disbursed prior to July 1, 2026, the program of study refers to their specific academic program at Syracuse University.
Example: If a student enrolled in an MBA program at Syracuse University before July 1, 2026, they may maintain legacy status if they remain in that MBA program.
However, if the student was enrolled in the MBA program at Syracuse University before July 1, 2026, and they change their graduate program to an M.S. in Psychology, or if the student transfers out of Syracuse University’s MBA program into another university’s MBA program, they will not maintain legacy status.

title: Admissions - College of Law – Syracuse University
description: Ready to join us? Check out our degree options to determine the right fit for you as you begin your legal education. From our On-Campus J.D. Program to our  ...
content:
Admissions - College of Law – Syracuse University
Check out our degree options to determine the right fit for you as you begin your legal education. From our On-Campus J.D. Program to our Online J.D. Program (JDinteractive), joint degree options, and masters/doctorate programs for foreign trained lawyers, we are here to prepare you for your future career in the law.
Take a tour of Dineen Hall, the home of Syracuse Law, through the eyes of a few of our students.
We host regular Open House webinars for our On-Campus J.D. and Online JDinteractive Programs, including an overview of the admissions process, a look inside our classrooms, and opportunities to ask questions.
Sign Up to Attend a Webinar
Our Admissions Team travels around the country and Canada to spread the word about Syracuse Law. Check out our recruitment calendar to see when we will be in your area!
We’d also love to host you in Syracuse, where you can sit in on a first-year class in the fall, winter, or spring, take a tour of Dineen Hall with one of our Law Ambassadors, and meet with one of our team members to discuss your potential future at Syracuse Law. Come visit us on a weekday soon.

Housing for First-Year Students
Living in a residence hall on campus keeps you close to all the action and provides a convenient home base for studying and relaxing during your first year.
Housing for Your Second Year and Beyond
Syracuse University provides housing options and assistance to fit a wide range of needs. About 53% of students move to the University's apartment-style housing on South Campus in their second year.

Residential Facilities and Apartments

title: Living On Campus - Syracuse University
description: Living on campus is an exciting experience that gives you access to all that campus has to offer.
content:
Living On Campus - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
Living on campus is an exciting part of your Syracuse University experience. You’ll have easy access to all that our campus offers, including classrooms, academic resources, dining halls, clubs and organizations and many other facilities and amenities.
Live and Learn in a Place Where You Can Be Yourself and Belong to Something Bigger
Over half of our students live on campus. This helps you build stronger connections with faculty and staff, deepen relationships with fellow students and learn what it means to be part of a campus community.
As a student, you can choose from a range of housing options, including traditional double rooms in residence halls, apartment-style living on South Campus after your second year or take part in a living learning community where students with shared interests or majors come together.
of Students Live on Campus.
Living Learning Communities
Syracuse University is more than just a beautiful place to live—we're a welcoming community where you’ll make lifelong friends and share life-changing experiences. From residential facilities to apartments, learn about housing options for first-year students, second-year students and beyond.
No matter where you are on campus, delicious, nutritious and convenient food options are always nearby. Our award-winning food services provides creative menus designed to delight your taste buds and fuel your mind and body. Learn about a wide variety of healthy food choices, menu plans, dining locations and nutritional information.
With a mix of traditional and contemporary architecture, sprawling greens and historic sculptures, the Syracuse University campus is more than just a backdrop. It’s the place where you’ll make lifelong friends and share life-changing experiences.
Transportation and Campus Shuttle
Our comprehensive and efficient shuttle service can take you to locations throughout the Syracuse University campus, and safety escorts are available on-call for night-time activities (or any time you’d like added security). And, the Connective Corridor makes it easy to get from campus to many of the city’s top destinations.

title: Living Off Campus - Syracuse University
description: Cultivate a spirit of freedom and independence by choosing off-campus living at Syracuse University and explore all the Syracuse area has to offer.
content:
Living Off Campus - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
Cultivate a spirit of freedom and independence by choosing off-campus living at Syracuse University and explore all the Syracuse area has to offer.
Off-Campus Living Opportunities
Living off campus provides you with the opportunity to be more independent with the benefit of University support. You can get involved with campus life and enjoy a positive college experience. Moving off campus is permitted once you've fulfilled the two-year housing requirement.
Tips When Considering Off-Campus Housing
When making the decision to move off campus, be thorough in your preparation and search process. Below are some highlights, and you can find complete details on the
Finding a place to live off campus can be both an exciting and stressful experience. The Apartment and Off-Campus Student Living team hosts the
Off-Campus Housing Search website
. This resource supports expediting the off-campus housing search process in addition to connecting Syracuse University students seeking roommates and subleasing opportunities.
Carefully review the lease before signing, as it will include everything expected of the tenant and details of what the landlord will provide. Undergraduate students have access to Student Legal Services as part of their student fee and are encouraged to have the lease reviewed by an attorney prior to signing. Visit the
Student Living Lease Signing Process
webpage for complete details.
Living off campus means an increased responsibility for personal safety. This includes personal safety such as utilizing services like the
, which provide free transportation on and around campus, in addition to property safety such as understanding fire and substance (e.g. carbon monoxide, lead and mold) best practices. Visit the Student Living
Read more tips for living off campus

title: Campus Highlights - Syracuse University
description: Traditional and contemporary architecture and sprawling green spaces make Syracuse's campus not only a place to live, but a place to build memories.
content:
Campus Highlights - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
With its mix of traditional and contemporary architecture, sprawling greens and historic sculptures, our campus is more than just a backdrop. It’s the place where you’ll make lifelong friends and share life-changing experiences.
At Syracuse University, all four seasons provide a rotating stage for year-round festivals, expos and athletic events that bring out the Orange spirit of our community. Our campus is big enough to lose yourself in your interests, but small enough that you’ll soon feel at home.
Here you’ll learn a little more about some of our favorite places on campus, but planning a visit is the best way to get a feel for a place that will soon feel like home.
Learn about some of our favorite places on campus and how they enhance the experiences of our students and community.
The Schine and Goldstein student centers are welcoming places designed for all kinds of student activities. Both locations offer flexible spaces, services and features for you to study, relax, meet up and enjoy a variety of dining options.
Learn more about student centers
As the heart of Syracuse University, Hendricks Chapel provides a sanctuary for all in our community to embrace and elevate religious, spiritual, moral and ethical principles. With a focus on belonging and celebrating the whole person, Hendricks Chapel welcomes everyone in the Syracuse University campus community.
Students experience the University firsthand from different and powerful perspectives. Student-run media provide more than just an opportunity for budding journalists—they offer platforms by students for students to share stories and insights on what's happening in the community and what lies ahead.
Learn more about student-run media
Whether you're a performer or a champion of the arts, there are plenty of chances to enjoy music, theater, comedy and dance on campus year-round.
Additional Campus Highlights
The University’s campus store is a primary resource for textbooks, course materials, general interest books, and art and office supplies. It’s also home to a full Apple retailer (and plenty of PC resources), a quick-stop grocery store, coffee bar and plenty of apparel—all the essentials to make living here a little more like home.
With 20 NCAA Division I men’s and women’s athletic teams, our Orange spirit unites and energizes our community like no other. Home to the Syracuse Orange football, basketball and lacrosse teams, the JMA Wireless Dome hosts some of the most memorable concerts, championships and events in the region and brings our community together to raise our voices and lift our spirits.
Explore the JMA Wireless Dome
Research, experiential learning and creative expression are core to the student experience at Syracuse University. Libraries across campus provide endless opportunities to discover and deepen virtually any interest.
Students join a fraternity or sorority for greater fellowship, to build lifelong connections and to get involved. With more than 50+ Greek organizations to choose from, it’s easy to find a community within Greek Life that matches your interests and goals.

title: Clubs and Organizations - Syracuse University
description: We have more than 300 extracurricular groups to covers every interest you can think of—academic, cultural, political, professional, recreational and more.
content:
Clubs and Organizations - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
Life and learning happen inside the classroom and out. We are home to more than 300 extracurricular groups, so you’ll never run out of ways to live and learn.
Whether you’ve got a passion for politics, a love of literature, or you’re just looking for a fun and interesting way to make new friends, student organizations are a great way to explore your interests and get involved. With more than 300 recognized student organizations on campus, our community covers every interest you can think of—academic, cultural, political, professional, recreational and more.
Joining a club at Syracuse University is a great way to learn new things, hone your skills and connect with others. With so many clubs around campus, it’s easy to find your community and explore new ones.
Student organizations, also known as registered student organizations (RSO), provide the space for you to learn, grow and network while exploring your academic and personal interests. Whether you’re an undergraduate or graduate student, it’s easy to find an RSO that fits your pursuits, or you can propose starting one of your own.
Get involved in the community. In addition to a world-class education, Syracuse University offers so many extracurricular opportunities to develop skills that will serve you well as you enter the real world.
NFL Management Council, General Counsel at National Football League

As the one-stop hub for student wellness, the

At the Barnes Center at The Arch, we take a holistic approach to wellness that encompasses mind, body, spirit and community. Through a variety of activities, resources and services available, we embolden all to explore and expand their health and wellness interests and goals.
I firmly believe the body and mind are one. The resources on campus like student clubs, professors and the Barnes Center at the Arch help students take care of their bodies and minds.
Health and Exercise Science B.S.

Our services and resources are organized placing individual student needs at the forefront. No matter where a student engages with the Barnes Center, they are empowered and encouraged to "Be Well" in the way that best meets their needs.

title: Belonging and Access - Syracuse University
description: Syracuse University is committed to fostering a welcoming environment for students, faculty and staff.
content:
Belonging and Access - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
We have a history of believing everyone’s contributions matter, which is why we continue to be committed to creating a welcoming environment for all.
Belonging at Syracuse University
We are committed to fostering a culture of that helps all students find connection and community.
The University recognizes that an enriching educational environment is one where individuals from varied backgrounds, perspectives and experiences come together to learn and grow. Through initiatives, programs and policies, we strive to create an atmosphere where every member of our campus community feels valued, respected and empowered.
This commitment extends across academic, social, and cultural dimensions, ensuring that students, faculty, and staff contribute to a collective learning experience that prepares individuals to thrive in a globally interconnected world.
Below are some of the ways that we seek to cultivate a community where differences of perspectives, backgrounds and experiences are both elevated and celebrated.
We are committed to fostering a respectful campus community, not only in vision but in practice. Our commitment is rooted in the belief that multiple points of view, life experiences, ethnicities, cultures and belief systems are essential to academic excellence.
Learn about our commitment
Opportunity should be open and accessible to everyone. Our extensive programs, services and resources recognize the potential in all of us by opening pathways to educational and professional pursuits.
Learn more about accessibility
Welcoming and Accessible Spaces
Across campus, we provide a variety of spaces and services that serve as gathering places for all students where they are encouraged to share their perspectives and experiences within these spaces.
Explore the spaces and services
It’s important to have resources that are accessible to different demographics.
College of Visual and Performing Arts
Every student deserves the opportunity to have a college experience that furthers their dreams. By creating a fully inclusive setting, InclusiveU provides pathways for students with intellectual and developmental disabilities to have a full and vibrant college experience at Syracuse University. From classes and coursework to sporting and social events, we offer opportunities for students to participate in every aspect of campus life and become vital members of our Orange community.
Learn more about InclusiveU
Center for Disability Resources
The Center for Disability Resources (CDR) provides a platform that supports students and strengthens inclusion in our community. From reshaping built environments to providing assistive technology, the center seeks to advance universal design and full inclusion to foster independent, self-determined learners.
Disability Cultural Center
The Disability Cultural Center coordinates campus-wide social, educational and cultural activities on disability issues for students, faculty, staff and community members with and without disabilities.
Center on Disability and Inclusion
The Center on Disability and Inclusion helps make schools, workplaces, and communities more welcoming for people with disabilities. Through research and outreach, the center works to create a more inclusive world—starting on campus and reaching around the globe.
Syracuse University has a long tradition of creating, promoting and enhancing a rich campus community. Browse the timeline below to learn more about the our legacy of expanding opportunities for all.
March 24, 1870 |  Promotes equal education for men and women
From the beginning, Syracuse University was founded on the principle of promoting equal education for men and women.
Aug. 16, 1943 | Maxwell School welcomes its first female professor
Marguerite J. Fisher G’42 (Ph.D.) becomes the first female professor at the Maxwell School.
June 8, 1948 | Syracuse University enrolls more veterans through the GI Bill than any other New York university
After the Servicemen’s Readjustment Act of 1944 (better known as the GI Bill), veteran enrollment at Syracuse University soared. Almost 10,000 veterans were enrolled during the 1947-48 school year. Though small, the University enrolled more veterans through the GI Bill than any other university in New York.
Learn more about VA education benefits information
May 12, 1971 |  Establishes an intercollegiate athletics program for women
Syracuse University establishes an intercollegiate athletics program for women.
Oct. 1, 2001 | LGBTQ Resource Center opens
Since its inception, the LGBTQ+ Resource Center has endeavored to build community within and beyond campus, raise awareness of LGBTQ+ identities and experiences, offer support to LGBTQ+ campus community members, and build a safer campus for all. The staff pursues these goals by planning and executing a number of annual events, including LGBTQ+ History Month, Lavender Graduation, HalloQueen Ball, Trans Week of Liberation and Queer Prom.
Learn more about the LGBTQ+ Resource Center
Oct. 19, 2001 | Maxwell unveils wall display honoring Syracuse University women
The Maxwell School of Citizenship and Public Affairs unveiled its permanent wall display honoring the women of Syracuse University of 1870-2000 in the Marguerite J. Fisher Seminar Room, 012 Eggers Hall.
May 17, 2011 | Disability Cultural Center opening announced
Syracuse University announces the founding of the Disability Cultural Center. Since its official opening in October 2012, the DCC has served and engaged faculty, staff, students, alumni, community members and others
Learn more about the Disability Cultural Center (DCC)
Oct. 11, 2016 | Indigenous Peoples' Day celebrated on campus and in the community
Syracuse University celebrates its first Indigenous Peoples’ Day to recognize and honor the history, cultures and contributions of Indigenous peoples and to raise awareness and generate dialogue across the campus community.
Learn more information about Native American and Indigenous students

title: Veteran and Military Student Support - Syracuse University
description: Syracuse University has a longstanding commitment to veterans and their families, providing the space and resources for exploring education and their futures.
content:
Veteran and Military Student Support - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
With a longstanding commitment to veteran and military students and their families, we provide you the space and resources to make an immediate impact on your career and community.
Where Veterans Learn and Lead
Join a community that honors your service and understands your experience. We ensure you have access to all that you need—from financial aid to mental health support to specialized resources—so you thrive while here and well beyond.
Whether it’s during the application process or throughout the duration of your program, our dedication to helping veteran and military-connected students succeed is why our University has been ranked one of the best private schools for veterans.
As one of the best private schools for veterans, we offer a blend of innovative educational and entrepreneurial programs to accelerate personal and professional growth.
Because of the amount of support that the University provides, it really facilitated an easy transition from the active-duty military into an advanced education.
Public Administration M.P.A and Law J.D.
College of Law and Maxwell School of Citizenship and Public Affairs
Transitioning service members, veterans and military spouses bring valuable skills and experience to civilian careers, and we are dedicated to helping you choose your path to your future.
Flexible Education Opportunities
Syracuse University has a variety of academic paths to meet your needs. Whether you’re looking to complete a professional certificate or a degree program, the flexibility of our programs allows you to pursue what matters most on your terms and timeline. And with more than 600 undergraduate, graduate and certificate programs across our schools and colleges, here you can personalize your path to the future.
We are committed to supporting veterans and their families as they explore and expand their entrepreneurial goals. Networking opportunities, bootcamps and training sessions provide the resources that can help when starting a business or venturing into new opportunities.
Additional Veteran and Military Family Support Resources
Office of Veteran and Military Affairs
The Office of Veteran and Military Affairs (OVMA) serves as the University’s single point of entry for all veteran and military-connected students, programs and initiatives.
D'Aniello Institute for Veterans and Military Families (IVMF)
The IVMF at Syracuse University is higher education’s first interdisciplinary academic institute, singularly focused on advancing the post-service lives of the nation’s military veterans and their families to serve those who have served.
With more than 200 options for advanced study as well as internships and research experiences, Syracuse University matches your ambition with opportunity. Whatever your interest, there’s a pathway here to pursue it.
The Office of Veteran and Military Affairs has established the VLF to support all programs that benefit our veteran and military-connected students on campus.

content:
Student Services - Syracuse University

Through Student Services, our staff and faculty strive to be responsive to the community's needs and create a safe, inclusive and engaging environment for all.
From career counseling to safety practices, students and parents are encouraged to take advantage of the variety of programs and services we offer. We welcome your questions and the opportunity to lend support so that we can become a stronger community, together.

From one-on-one coaching to events, explore resources that can help improve your financial literacy and achieve your short- and long-term goals.

The Department of Public Safety brings together a police force, Students for Community Safety, Fire and Life Services and emergency management to ensure that the Syracuse community is a safe place to live and learn.

Our comprehensive and efficient shuttle service can take you to locations throughout campus, while safety escorts are available on-call for nighttime activities (or any time you’d like added security).

title: Student Centers - Syracuse University
description: ​The Schine and Goldstein Student Centers are two dedicated buildings that host student activities of every kind.
content:
Student Centers - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
The Schine and Goldstein student centers are welcoming places where you can study, relax, eat, gather with friends, collaborate on projects and take advantage of a variety of services.
The newly renovated Hildegarde and J. Myer Schine Student Center is the University’s living room. As the hub for student life, Schine is the place to meet, eat, relax, study and attend events. It features open and accessible spaces and a central atrium with a two-story video wall.
The Ann and Alfred Goldstein Student Center is a 33,000 square-foot facility located on South Campus. It features a dining center, a convenience store, computer cluster, laundry room, fitness center, video games and a quiet study lounge.
The Barnes Center at The Arch
As the hub for student wellness, the Barnes Center at The Arch features programs, services and offerings that promote holistic health and well-being, all in one accessible, centralized space on campus.
The Intercultural Collective
The Intercultural Collective is a collaboration of the Office of Multicultural Affairs, the Disability Cultural Center and the LGBTQ Resource Center. Located in the Schine Student Center, the collective serves as a communal hub where you can connect with other students.
Learn about the Intercultural Collective
With quiet study spaces, group meeting rooms, wireless access, 24/7 assistance and over 4 million books, journals and other items,
Syracuse University Libraries
supports research and scholarship throughout campus and beyond.
Center for Student Excellence
The Center for Student Excellence (CSE) is a centralized hub for student success, bringing together multiple support offices under one coordinated structure. From their first day on campus through graduation, the center helps students thrive academically, personally and professionally by providing clear pathways to academic support, engagement and persistence resources—all in one place.
Discover the Center for Student Excellence
Additional Student Centers Resources
Student Engagement serves as a resource and information network supporting the Schine and Goldstein student centers, registered student organizations, non-academic events and student development programs centered on leadership, connection and community.
Explore student engagement
Student Center Hours, Reservations and Programs
Student Centers and Programming Services (SCPS) manages the Schine and Goldstein student centers and supports student, academic and co-curricular programs and events.
Syracuse University Events
Syracuse University offers a vibrant array of campus events that support your academic, intellectual and personal development.

content:
How a Brand Builder Thinks - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
Syracuse University alumnus spreads success through marketing and mentoring.
When Rah Mahtani ’10 was weighing his college options, he found the perfect fit for his interests at Syracuse University: a nationally ranked industrial design program in the
College of Visual and Performing Arts
, big-time college athletics and a vibrant campus atmosphere. “Syracuse University had the elements of a very beautiful, tightly knit campus and an amazing feel,” he says. “It hit all the right notes for me.”
Today, as the head of marketing and commercial strategy, U.S., for Alibaba.com—the world’s largest business-to-business wholesale marketplace—Mahtani is recognized as a thought leader in marketing and digital communications, and he regularly draws on lessons and experiences from his time at Syracuse that have shaped his professional success. He credits the industrial design program (now
industrial and interaction design
) for introducing him to people-first thinking that focuses on meeting consumers, understanding their motivations and how they might use a product. “I leverage that thinking in marketing campaigns and mentoring businesses daily,” he says. “Everything I learned at Syracuse University plus the connections I made have all translated into really incredible things for me.”
A self-described “sneakerhead,” basketball fan and auto enthusiast, Mahtani envisioned a career designing sneakers and enjoyed an array of activities at Syracuse. He got fired up for Orange basketball games and had fun on camping trips. As a budding entrepreneur, he teamed up with industrial designer and former faculty member Rob Englert to launch an apparel company. He also was a member of the University marketing and communications’ first social media team. “That gave me my first taste of the world of social media,” he says.
Sharing a Flair for Social Media
Rah Mahtani ’10, a College of Visual and Performing Arts alumnus, discusses his career path with students at the Syracuse University Libraries’ LaunchPad.
After graduating, Mahtani entered a challenging job market, but his social media skills and passion for cars paved the way to employment. “When I had the opportunity to work in the car world, I jumped at it and learned as much as I possibly could from being in that universe,” he says.
Mahtani joined a social media marketing agency that did work for Chevrolet and parlayed that experience into becoming Volvo’s social media manager. From there, he advanced through social media and digital and marketing communications positions with Jack in the Box and MINI Cooper, BMW of North America. In 2023, he was named head of marketing for North America for Alibaba.com—a subsidiary of the Alibaba Group, a Chinese-owned multinational technology company—and moved into his current position last year.
“I talk to entrepreneurs and business owners all the time and it’s so inspiring. It’s incredible to see that they had a vision and acted on it because most people are afraid of that initial leap,” he says. “Small business owners are the backbone of the economy, so I’m super fortunate to speak to them every day and then work on building products or marketing campaigns that speak directly to them—so I just feel lucky.”
Everything I learned at Syracuse University plus the connections I made have all translated into really incredible things for me.
His work has generated plenty of buzz, too. He’s collaborated with Martha Stewart, the Shark Tank’s Daymond John and Snoop Dogg, whom he partnered with to create the “Merry Munchie Meal” (price: $4.20)—a PR coup for Jack in the Box when California legalized cannabis. “It was interesting to see how someone like Snoop who’s that accomplished was so involved in the minute details,” he says.
Mahtani also guided the burger franchise into the Guinness Book of World Records, creating the world’s largest coupon that served up a buy-one-get-one-free burger offer. In 2019, he orchestrated hanging a MINI Cooper on the side of the Los Angeles Convention Center during the annual LA Auto Show.
Instilling Entrepreneurial Spirit
Mahtani shares insights with students in Professor Don Carr’s Sustainable Product Systems class. Mahtani credits Carr and other members of the industrial and interaction design faculty for shaping his people-first thinking about design.
During a campus visit in December, Mahtani met with students at
Syracuse University Libraries’ LaunchPad
and in Professor Don Carr’s Sustainable Product Systems class. He highlighted his career experiences, marketing campaigns and the importance of understanding a product’s users. He shared career advice, discussed sustainability and the appropriate use of artificial intelligence. He also described his management style, noting the value he places on being open and building trust with employees.
Mahtani talked about his work with Alibaba.com, where he advises and mentors small business owners, inventors, entrepreneurs and the e-commerce community on developing products and bringing them to market, as well as supply-chain sourcing and other issues. He also discussed China’s manufacturing infrastructure and role at the center of the global supply chain—and his focus on shifting the American mindset on Chinese products.
Mahtani hosts COCREATE, Alibaba.com’s flagship business-to-business conference. Held in Las Vegas and London, the event features the world’s largest product-based pitch competition, and Mahtani shared recent winners’ product videos with the students, emphasizing the power of a strong pitch. “Even if you have a good idea,” he said, “if you can’t clearly articulate the problem and the solution, then you’re probably wasting your time. You need to be able to do that really succinctly.”
Mahtani addresses the audience at the COCREATE 2025 conference in Las Vegas. It was his third year hosting the event, which includes the planet’s leading pitch-based competition, drawing 25,000 applicants with the winner taking home $200,000.
For Mahtani, building brands and leading marketing campaigns requires messaging that people notice. “Social media governs or influences everything I’ve done in my career,” he says. “I think social media first in terms of the speed of getting the message across for any sort of campaign, but also in terms of the fact that you really need to capture attention fast.”
That expertise and his commitment to human-centered design have proven influential. “I’m fortunate my career kind of came full circle,” he says, “but I still never got to put pen to paper and sketch out something incredible that came to market.”
Instead, he’s leading others on that journey, with a vision for success.
Delivering the Perfect Pitch
Student entrepreneurs champion their startups in challenging competitions.
Collaborating for a Clean Future
How a community-first partnership is preparing the next generation to protect the environment and public health.

title: Living Learning Communities - Syracuse University
description: Living learning communities allow students to live and take classes with peers who share common interests and majors.
content:
Living Learning Communities - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
Our living learning communities allow you to live and take classes with a group of students who share a common interest or academic major.
Why Living Learning Communities?
You’ll develop skills and connections that support your academic achievement, a better understanding of yourself and others, and a greater ability to balance your academic and social life. This eases your transition to college life and provides you with a group of peers and friends who become like a family away from home.
More than 25+ living learning communities are offered, grouped under the broad categories of:
Cultural exploration and diversity
Leadership and citizenship
Science, technology, engineering and math
How to Request a Living Learning Community
Indicate your living learning community choice(s) on the online Housing and Meal Plan Application. If you are interested in more than one, rank your choices to a maximum of three. If you are also making a mutual roommate request, each of you must make identical rankings. There is no additional housing cost associated with joining a living learning community.
If you have been invited to participate in the Renée Crown University Honors Program, you are encouraged to enroll in any living learning community. Honors students who do not select one of the other living learning communities will be automatically housed in proximity to other honors students in clusters collectively known as the Honors Learning Community.
Join a living learning community
Students Share Their Experiences
Aubrey Williams '28 and Amanda DeFeo '28 sit down to talk about Living Learning Communities.
There are so many resources offered to Living Learning Community students that many other students don’t get access to. Take advantage of what’s being offered to you even if you aren’t sure how it could help you right now.
Environmental Engineering B.S.
College of Engineering and Computer Science
FAQs About Living Learning Communities
Get answers to all your questions about living in a living learning community.
Housing at Syracuse University

title: ​Safety and Security - Syracuse University
description: Syracuse University is dedicated to maintaining a safe and comfortable environment for students, faculty, and staff.
content:
​Safety and Security - Syracuse University
By continuing to use this site, you agree to the use of cookies in accordance with our
The Department of Public Safety (DPS) is dedicated to maintaining an environment where you feel at home. A big part of that is making sure you are safe and feel comfortable, wherever you are.
DPS brings together a police force, Students for Community Safety, Fire and Life Services, and emergency management to ensure that the Syracuse community is a safe place to live and learn.
DPS's goal is to create a safe and secure living, learning, social and cultural environment. In addition to on- and off-campus patrols, DPS supports community safety through:
blue light emergency phone system
crisis notification system
to share information, collaborate on crime prevention and respond to community needs
Every residence hall on campus has security aides on duty day and night, secured entrances and video security cameras. All visitors must check-in at the front desk, and overnight guests must be registered in advance. Off campus, DPS partners with the City of Syracuse Police on neighborhood safety patrols. Our proximity to the VA Hospital, SUNY Upstate Medical University, SUNY College of Environmental Science and Forestry, and the Syracuse Police Department makes us accessible to multiple emergency response resources. In short, our campus is one of the safest places you can be.
with your questions about campus safety; DPS staff will be happy to share more information and answer your questions. And be sure to download the
. It enables you to communicate with DPS, share your location in the event of an emergency and let your friends know you've safely made it home.

title: Resources - Graduate – Syracuse University
content:
Resources - Graduate – Syracuse University
A multitude of resources are available to assist graduate and professional students in meeting the academic, personal and professional challenges they face.

title: Graduate and Professional Programs - Graduate – Syracuse University
content:
Graduate and Professional Programs - Graduate – Syracuse University
Syracuse University is home to some of the top graduate and professional programs in the country for a reason—because we substantially invest in research.
As a graduate or professional student, you’ll work towards breakthroughs alongside faculty who are among the world’s finest researchers and scholars, all while creating lifelong professional relationships and advancing your career.
Ranked among the top 55 universities in the United States, you’ll join an international community of masters, doctoral, and professional students in an environment that’s equal parts nurturing and challenging. It’s the perfect environment for you to take your next step.
240 advanced degree and credential
programs from across Syracuse’s 13 schools and colleges, then customize your education with a rich selection of courses, internships and research projects.
Syracuse University has offered online degree programs for more than 20 years, long recognizing the needs of working professionals, veterans, and other students who have commitments off campus.
Once you’ve selected a program of interest, be sure to note the application deadline.  Programs starting in the summer term have earlier deadlines than those programs that begin in the fall.  Spring starts may not be available for many residential programs.
Contact Graduate Admissions
Contacting the school or college that offers your program of interest will be helpful in obtaining specific information needed for the application and admission process, and learning about funding opportunities.
Graduate Recruiting Events
Syracuse University admissions representatives travel to graduate school fairs and tours across the U.S. and around the world.  Meet with them when in your area to learn more about our University.

title: Class and Classroom Scheduling - Office of the Registrar – Syracuse University
content:
Class and Classroom Scheduling - Office of the Registrar – Syracuse University
The goal of class and classroom scheduling is to make the most efficient and effective use of instructional days and classroom space to support the academic mission of Syracuse University.
Syracuse University Class/Classroom Scheduling and Contact Hours Policy
Class Scheduling Paradigm.
To make the most efficient and effective use of instructional days and classroom space to support a positive academic and instructional experience at Syracuse University
Apply classroom assignments in a consistent and equitable manner across departments and colleges, while maintaining flexibility to accommodate special needs and circumstances.
Minimize time conflicts in class offerings to facilitate students’ registration both for required courses and for inter-disciplinary sampling of courses.
Allow for exceptions deemed to be in the University’s best interests by the University Registrar and Associate Provost.
Classroom assignments are based classroom space, current enrollment, seating capacity, classroom technology, instructional requirements and special needs or instructions provided by the department/school or college.
Classroom assignments are processed in the following order:
Classes meeting in the approved
Classes meeting one day per week for 2 hours and 45 minutes
Discussions and recitations
Classes approved for a meeting pattern exception
Note: Room assignments will not be confirmed until the first day of classes
. The relocation of classroom assignments before the start of a term may occur to meet enrollment fluctuations, and changes to faculty assignments.
A change to the day or time offering of a class will be prohibited after enrollment has occurred.
Once the process of assigning classrooms for an upcoming term has occurred, Registrar classrooms may be available for other uses. Student groups and organizations or departments and colleges requesting a classroom for non-academic purposes submit requests to

title: Diplomas - Office of the Registrar – Syracuse University
content:
Diplomas - Office of the Registrar – Syracuse University
Four steps for graduating students:
File for Diploma through your MySlice account.
for the most current information about the upcoming Commencement ceremony.
First Destination Survey on Handshake
Visit the campus Bookstore for cap and gown orders.
Getting ready to graduate? Tell us!
Graduating students must notify the University that they intend to graduate through the File Diploma Request process, accessed through MySlice. Only students who complete this process are included in degree certification review. Commencement information is only sent to students who have filed a diploma request.
Log in to MySlice > Student Home > Academics > Diploma Request. Specify the term (fall, spring, or summer) and provide information for the diploma, including a mailing address.
Complete this process prior to the start of your final semester and also contact your home school or college to review all graduation requirements.
establishes deadlines to file for graduation, published on their website as
. Also consult your department regarding deadlines and for information regarding completion of requirements.
Each undergraduate school and college certifies the completion of its students’ degree requirements. Minors are also certified by a student’s school/college. For graduate students, certification of degrees and certificates of advanced study may be handled by their department or by the Graduate Certification office in 304 Lyman Hall. The certification process generally takes four to six weeks after the completion of requirements.
Once certification is complete, the Registrar’s Office posts the award and orders the diploma. When posted, the degree appears on the academic transcript. Minors also appear on the transcript once the degree is posted and the minor is certified as complete.
Syracuse University awards degrees and certificates four times per year: in May, June or July, August, and December. Students’ degrees are awarded for the award date that falls on or after the date on which all degree requirements were completed.
Graduation and Commencement
Syracuse University has one graduation ceremony each year, in May.
Only students who file a diploma request will receive information about commencement. See the
for more information about eligibility, dates, activities, etc.
Other information related to commencement, such as cap and gown ordering, can be found through the
Syracuse University has authorized the National Student Clearinghouse to provide
degree and enrollment verification
After a degree or certificate of advanced study (C.A.S.) is awarded, diplomas and certificates are ordered for students who have cleared all holds from the Office of Academic Integrity and Community Standards as well as any financial obligations to the University. Diplomas and certificates are mailed to the address provided in the file diploma request process (see above).
Order a Diploma Replacement and eDiploma Online
The following services are available online through our official diploma vendor, the
Order a replacement diploma – Order your diploma easily online. You will be able to choose Standard Delivery or Express Overnight Delivery. There is a $15.00 charge for Bachelor’s, Master’s, and certificates. Doctoral and JD replacements are $20.00. Your Diploma will be printed and mailed within 1-2 days of your order.
Order an Official eDiploma – Your Syracuse University eDiploma is a Signed and Certified PDF of your original paper diploma used for easy diploma verification purposes. The Syracuse University eDiploma is a one-time charge of $5. You will receive your eDiploma within minutes of ordering. You can then share your eDiploma as often as you need.
Diploma Status – Search the status of your diploma order whether it is your new diploma or replacement diploma.
